head_coach.name: John Harbaugh
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Physical, disciplined football rooted in Andy Reid Philadelphia coaching tree. Defensive-minded HC who presided over historically elite defenses in Baltimore. Values running the football, aggressive defense, and top-tier special teams. Adaptable — oversaw both run-heavy (Lamar Jackson era) and pass-first offenses in Baltimore.

defensive_coordinator.name: Dennard Wilson
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Todd Bowles / Gregg Williams aggressive blitz lineage, refined under Mike Macdonald (Baltimore Ravens 2023) and Vic Fangio-adjacent Eagles 2022. DB coaching roots.

offense.scheme_family: Reid West Coast (modernized with spread/RPO elements)
offense.base_personnel: 11 (with significant 12 personnel usage; 2025 Giants ran 12 personnel at season-high 40% in some games)
offense.run_scheme: Mixed: Greg Roman (senior offensive assistant / de facto run game coordinator) brings power, gap, and duo schemes. Roman's offenses historically top-4 in rushing yards 9 of 12 years as OC. Expect inside zone, power, counter, and QB-designed runs for Jaxson Dart. Reid-tree RPO concepts layered on top.
offense.pass_concepts: Short-to-intermediate timing routes (slants, curls, digs, spacing, flood, sail) with play-action shots off run game. Heavy use of motion and pre-snap shifts. RPOs integrated throughout. Vertical passing game built off play-action and bootlegs rather than pure dropback. Shotgun-heavy (~75%+).
offense.rpo_usage: high — Reid-tree offenses have heavily incorporated RPOs since 2016; Nagy referenced maintaining 'the RPO world' as central to KC's scheme
offense.te_usage: Significant receiving weapon. Kansas City historically had the NFL's second-highest TE target rate. Theo Johnson had 45 catches, 528 yards, team-high 5 TDs in 2025 and was a key red zone target (22% RZ target share). Expect TE flexed into slot, used on seam routes, crossing patterns, and in 12 personnel sets. Danny Breyer (former Ravens TE guru) hired as pass game coordinator, further emphasizing TE receiving role.
offense.rb_usage: Dual-threat emphasis. Reid-tree WCO historically targets RBs heavily in passing game (McCoy, Westbrook, Charles all 4-5+ targets/game). Cam Skattebo's receiving skills were explicitly noted as a weapon. Tyrone Tracy also involved. Greg Roman's influence adds a physical, power-run dimension. Committee approach likely.

defense.alignment: Odd front (3-down linemen with stand-up OLBs), frequently shifts to 2-4-5 Nickel (~70% of snaps in Tennessee). Wilson ran a 'Ravens-Lite' hybrid 3-4 that functioned as a 2-4-5 on passing downs.
defense.edge_rush_source: Hybrid — DEs (Brian Burns, Abdul Carter) are primary edge rushers who can play with hand in dirt or standing up. Burns plays as a traditional 4-3 DE type. Carter (2025 3rd overall pick, Penn State LB/EDGE hybrid) is a stand-up rusher. Wilson's scheme uses simulated pressures with 6-man blitz looks that drop 2 into coverage. Giants also have Dexter Lawrence as an elite interior pass rusher from the nose.
defense.coverage_base: Cover 4 (quarters) primary — Wilson's Titans ran Cover 4 on 21% of coverages, with heavy zone emphasis (75% zone, 18% man in 2025). Also uses Cover 3 to close off middle of field. Split-safety (two-high) shells protect pressing corners. Simulated pressures are a hallmark — showing blitz with 6 but only rushing 4.
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate-low (Wilson's Titans blitzed ~20.5-20.9% in 2024-2025, ranked 23rd). Wilson prefers simulated pressures over true blitzes. He stated blitzing will happen 'at the right times' rather than constantly.
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Bobby Okereke) are coverage/run hybrid players in this scheme. OLBs (Abdul Carter, potentially Kayvon Thibodeaux) are primarily pass rushers who function as edge defenders. Wilson's 3-4 creates situations where stand-up OLBs ARE the primary edge rush.
defense.safety_usage: Wilson's background as a DB coach means safeties are used in multiple roles. He developed Kyle Hamilton into an All-Pro hybrid safety in Baltimore (2023). Wilson uses split-safety shells heavily but values a strong safety who can play near the box in run support and cover TEs/slot receivers. Expect at least one safety used in a versatile, hybrid role.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: The Reid/Nagy offensive tree is the single most TE-friendly scheme family in the NFL. Travis Kelce's entire career was built in this system, where the TE functions as a de facto WR1 from flexed/slot alignments. Kansas City historically had the NFL's second-highest TE target rate. Theo Johnson already emerged as a red zone weapon (5 TDs, 22% RZ target share in 2025) and Danny Breyer was specifically hired as pass game coordinator to maximize TE receiving production. Any TE with elite route running, hands, and separation will be schemed into high-value receiving roles, functioning closer to a WR than a traditional inline blocker.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Wilson's 3-4 hybrid base creates direct LB-to-EDGE value conversion. The OLB position in this defense IS the primary edge rusher — Abdul Carter was drafted 3rd overall as a Penn State LB and is being deployed as a stand-up edge rusher. Wilson's scheme frequently uses 2-4-5 Nickel where stand-up OLBs rush off the edge. Any LB prospect with elite pass rush traits (burst, bend, first step) fits this defense as a premium pass rusher, not a traditional off-ball LB. Wilson also uses simulated pressures that create additional pass rush lanes for athletic LBs who can rush on designed blitzes.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Reid-tree West Coast offense historically demands pass-catching backs. Brian Westbrook caught 90 passes in this system; LeSean McCoy averaged nearly 5 targets per game under Reid. Cam Skattebo was specifically lauded for his 'excellent receiving skills' as a weapon on passing downs. The addition of Greg Roman adds a power run element, but the passing game will still heavily feature the RB on screens, swing routes, flat routes, and option routes out of the backfield. A back with elite hands, route running, and pass protection fits this offense at a premium.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Wilson is a DB coach by trade who developed Kyle Hamilton into an All-Pro in Baltimore (2023), where Hamilton played as a true hybrid — box safety, slot defender, occasional blitzer, and deep coverage player. Wilson's scheme demands versatile safeties who can handle multiple roles within his split-safety shells and simulated pressure packages. A safety who can play in the box, cover slot receivers, and contribute to the blitz package has significantly elevated value in this defense. Wilson's Cover 4 base also requires safeties who can play aggressive run support from two-high looks.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: The 2026 Giants are a rare team where ALL FOUR positional value inflections rate high, creating maximum scheme-driven value for versatile prospects. On offense, the Nagy/Reid West Coast tree + Greg Roman run game creates a TE-as-WR system (Kelce prototype) and RB-as-receiver system (Westbrook/McCoy prototype). A receiving TE like Kenyon Sadiq should be valued near WR levels for NYG. On defense, Wilson's 3-4 hybrid with 2-4-5 Nickel tendencies means any LB with pass rush traits (Arvell Reese, Sonny Styles type) is valued as an EDGE rusher — Abdul Carter's own LB-to-EDGE conversion is the template. The defensive front already features Burns, Carter, and Dexter Lawrence, with Thibodeaux potentially being traded, so additional versatile front-seven athletes are coveted. Wilson's DB-coach pedigree and Kyle Hamilton development track record means hybrid safeties who can play slot/box/deep have premium value. NYG's 5th overall pick in 2026 is most likely to target a defensive front player (DL/EDGE) or a dynamic safety/versatile defender — Harbaugh explicitly wants a 'gold jacket player' at that pick.
